questions.prompt: Put the chain in the right order.
questions.explanation: A human error introduces a defect in the code, which produces a failure when executed.

questions.prompt: A flaw exists in the code but no test ever triggers it. What do you have?
options: A defect but no observed failure
Neither a defect nor a failure
questions.explanation: The defect is present; a failure only occurs when the triggering conditions execute. No observed failure doesn't mean no defect.

questions.prompt: A developer wrote < instead of <=. The < instead of <= is the:
questions.explanation: The wrong operator in the code is the defect; the human choosing it was the error; the wrong result a user sees is the failure.

questions.prompt: You retest a fix and the bug is still there. Which state does it move to?
questions.explanation: A fix that fails retest is Reopened and sent back to development.

questions.prompt: Why do bugs tend to cluster in a few modules?
options: Testers only look in those modules
Complexity and change concentrate there, so defect density does too
questions.explanation: The defect-clustering principle: a small number of complex, heavily-changed modules carry most defects, which helps target testing.
